1→---
     2→name: add-apm-integrations
     3→description: Write a new library instrumentation end-to-end. Use when the user ask to add a new APM integration or a library instrumentation.
     4→context: fork
     5→allowed-tools:
     6→  - Bash
     7→  - Read
     8→  - Write
     9→  - Edit
    10→  - Glob
    11→  - Grep
    12→---
    13→
    14→Write a new APM end-to-end integration for dd-trace-java, based on library instrumentations, following all project conventions.
    15→
    16→## Step 1 – Read the authoritative docs and sync this skill (mandatory, always first)
    17→
    18→Before writing any code, read all three files in full:
    19→
    20→1. [`docs/how_instrumentations_work.md`](docs/how_instrumentations_work.md) — full reference (types, methods, advice, helpers, context stores, decorators)
    21→2. [`docs/add_new_instrumentation.md`](docs/add_new_instrumentation.md) — step-by-step walkthrough
    22→3. [`docs/how_to_test.md`](docs/how_to_test.md) — test types and how to run them
    23→
    24→These files are the single source of truth. Reference them while implementing.
    25→
    26→**After reading the docs, sync this skill with them:**
    27→
    28→Compare the content of the three docs against the rules encoded in Steps 2–11 of this skill file. Look for:
    29→- Patterns, APIs, or conventions described in the docs but absent or incorrect here
    30→- Steps that are out of date relative to the current docs (e.g. renamed methods, new base classes)
    31→- Advice constraints or test requirements that have changed
    32→
    33→For every discrepancy found, edit this file (`.claude/skills/apm-integrations/SKILL.md`) to correct it using the
    34→`Edit` tool before continuing. Keep changes targeted: fix what diverged, add what is missing, remove what is wrong.
    35→Do not touch content that already matches the docs.
    36→
    37→## Step 2 – Clarify the task
    38→
    39→If the user has not already provided all of the following, ask before proceeding:
    40→
    41→- **Framework name** and **minimum supported version** (e.g. `okhttp-3.0`)
    42→- **Target class(es) and method(s)** to instrument (fully qualified class names preferred)
    43→- **Target system**: one of `Tracing`, `Profiling`, `AppSec`, `Iast`, `CiVisibility`, `Usm`, `ContextTracking`
    44→- **Whether this is a bootstrap instrumentation** (affects allowed imports)
    45→
    46→## Step 3 – Find a reference instrumentation
    47→
    48→Search `dd-java-agent/instrumentation/` for a structurally similar integration:
    49→- Same target system
    50→- Comparable type-matching strategy (single type, hierarchy, known types)
    51→
    52→Read the reference integration's `InstrumenterModule`, Advice, Decorator, and test files to understand the established
    53→pattern before writing new code. Use it as a template.
    54→
    55→## Step 4 – Set up the module
    56→
    57→1. Create directory: `dd-java-agent/instrumentation/$framework/$framework-$minVersion/`
    58→2. Under it, create the standard Maven source layout:
    59→   - `src/main/java/` — instrumentation code
    60→   - `src/test/groovy/` — Spock tests
    61→3. Create `build.gradle` with:
    62→   - `compileOnly` dependencies for the target framework
    63→   - `testImplementation` dependencies for tests
    64→   - `muzzle { pass { } }` directives (see Step 9)
    65→4. Register the new module in `settings.gradle.kts` in **alphabetical order**
    66→
    67→## Step 5 – Write the InstrumenterModule
    68→
    69→Conventions to enforce:
    70→
    71→- Add `@AutoService(InstrumenterModule.class)` annotation — required for auto-discovery
    72→- Extend the correct `InstrumenterModule.*` subclass (never the bare abstract class)
    73→- Implement the **narrowest** `Instrumenter` interface possible:
    74→  - Prefer `ForSingleType` > `ForKnownTypes` > `ForTypeHierarchy`
    75→- Add `classLoaderMatcher()` if a sentinel class identifies the framework on the classpath
    76→- Declare **all** helper class names in `helperClassNames()`:
    77→  - Include inner classes (`Foo$Bar`), anonymous classes (`Foo$1`), and enum synthetic classes
    78→- Declare `contextStore()` entries if context stores are needed (key class → value class)
    79→- Keep method matchers as narrow as possible (name, parameter types, visibility)
    80→
    81→## Step 6 – Write the Decorator
    82→
    83→- Extend the most specific available base decorator:
    84→  - `HttpClientDecorator`, `DatabaseClientDecorator`, `ServerDecorator`, `MessagingClientDecorator`, etc.
    85→- One `public static final DECORATE` instance
    86→- Define `UTF8BytesString` constants for the component name and operation name
    87→- Keep all tag/naming/error logic here — not in the Advice class
    88→- Override `spanType()`, `component()`, `spanKind()` as appropriate
    89→
    90→## Step 7 – Write the Advice class (highest-risk step)
    91→
    92→### Must do
    93→
    94→- Advice methods **must** be `static`
    95→- Annotate enter: `@Advice.OnMethodEnter(suppress = Throwable.class)`
    96→- Annotate exit: `@Advice.OnMethodExit(onThrowable = Throwable.class, suppress = Throwable.class)`
    97→  - **Exception**: do NOT use `suppress` when hooking a constructor
    98→- Use `@Advice.Local("...")` for values shared between enter and exit (span, scope)
    99→- Use the correct parameter annotations:
   100→  - `@Advice.This` — the receiver object
   101→  - `@Advice.Argument(N)` — a method argument by index
   102→  - `@Advice.Return` — the return value (exit only)
   103→  - `@Advice.Thrown` — the thrown exception (exit only)
   104→  - `@Advice.Enter` — the return value of the enter method (exit only)
   105→- Use `CallDepthThreadLocalMap` to guard against recursive instrumentation of the same method
   106→
   107→### Span lifecycle (in order)
   108→
   109→Enter method:
   110→1. `AgentSpan span = startSpan(DECORATE.operationName(), ...)`
   111→2. `DECORATE.afterStart(span)` + set domain-specific tags
   112→3. `AgentScope scope = activateSpan(span)` — return or store via `@Advice.Local`
   113→
   114→Exit method:
   115→4. `DECORATE.onError(span, throwable)` — only if throwable is non-null
   116→5. `DECORATE.beforeFinish(span)`
   117→6. `span.finish()`
   118→7. `scope.close()`
   119→
   120→### Must NOT do
   121→
   122→- **No logger fields** in the Advice class or the Instrumentation class (loggers only in helpers/decorators)
   123→- **No code in the Advice constructor** — it is never called
   124→- **Do not use lambdas in advice methods** — they create synthetic classes that will be missing from helper declarations
   125→- **No references** to other methods in the same Advice class or in the InstrumenterModule class
   126→- **No `InstrumentationContext.get()`** outside of Advice code
   127→- **No `inline=false`** in production code (only for debugging; must be removed before committing)
   128→- **No `java.util.logging.*`, `java.nio.*`, or `javax.management.*`** in bootstrap instrumentations
   129→
   130→## Step 8 – Add SETTER/GETTER adapters (if applicable)
   131→
   132→For context propagation to and from upstream services, like HTTP headers,
   133→implement `AgentPropagation.Setter` / `AgentPropagation.Getter` adapters that wrap the framework's specific header API.
   134→Place them in the helpers package, declare them in `helperClassNames()`.
   135→
   136→## Step 9 – Write tests
   137→
   138→Cover all mandatory test types:
   139→
   140→### 1. Instrumentation test (mandatory)
   141→
   142→- Spock spec extending `InstrumentationSpecification`
   143→- Place in `src/test/groovy/`
   144→- Verify: spans created, tags set, errors propagated, resource names correct
   145→- Use `TEST_WRITER.waitForTraces(N)` for assertions
   146→- Use `runUnderTrace("root") { ... }` for synchronous code
   147→
   148→For tests that need a separate JVM, suffix the test class with `ForkedTest` and run via the `forkedTest` task.
   149→
   150→### 2. Muzzle directives (mandatory)
   151→
   152→In `build.gradle`, add `muzzle` blocks:
   153→```groovy
   154→muzzle {
   155→  pass {
   156→    group = "com.example"
   157→    module = "framework"
   158→    versions = "[$minVersion,)"
   159→    assertInverse = true  // ensures versions below $minVersion fail muzzle
   160→  }
   161→}
   162→```
   163→
   164→### 3. Latest dependency test (mandatory)
   165→
   166→Use the `latestDepTestLibrary` helper in `build.gradle` to pin the latest available version. Run with:
   167→```bash
   168→./gradlew :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:$framework-$version:latestDepTest
   169→```
   170→
   171→### 4. Smoke test (optional)
   172→
   173→Add a smoke test in `dd-smoke-tests/` only if the framework warrants a full end-to-end demo-app test.
   174→
   175→## Step 10 – Build and verify
   176→
   177→Run these commands in order and fix any failures before proceeding:
   178→
   179→```bash
   180→./gradlew :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:$framework-$version:muzzle
   181→./gradlew :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:$framework-$version:test
   182→./gradlew :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:$framework-$version:latestDepTest
   183→./gradlew spotlessCheck
   184→```
   185→
   186→**If muzzle fails:** check for missing helper class names in `helperClassNames()`.
   187→
   188→**If tests fail:** verify span lifecycle order (start → activate → error → finish → close), helper registration,
   189→and `contextStore()` map entries match actual usage.
   190→
   191→**If spotlessCheck fails:** run `./gradlew spotlessApply` to auto-format, then re-check.
   192→
   193→## Step 11 – Checklist before finishing
   194→
   195→Output this checklist and confirm each item is satisfied:
   196→
   197→- [ ] `settings.gradle.kts` entry added in alphabetical order
   198→- [ ] `build.gradle` has `compileOnly` deps and `muzzle` directives with `assertInverse = true`
   199→- [ ] `@AutoService(InstrumenterModule.class)` annotation present on the module class
   200→- [ ] `helperClassNames()` lists ALL referenced helpers (including inner, anonymous, and enum synthetic classes)
   201→- [ ] Advice methods are `static` with `@Advice.OnMethodEnter` / `@Advice.OnMethodExit` annotations
   202→- [ ] `suppress = Throwable.class` on enter/exit (unless the hooked method is a constructor)
   203→- [ ] No logger field in the Advice class or InstrumenterModule class
   204→- [ ] No `inline=false` left in production code
   205→- [ ] No `java.util.logging.*` / `java.nio.*` / `javax.management.*` in bootstrap path
   206→- [ ] Span lifecycle order is correct: startSpan → afterStart → activateSpan (enter); onError → beforeFinish → finish → close (exit)
   207→- [ ] Muzzle passes
   208→- [ ] Instrumentation tests pass
   209→- [ ] `latestDepTest` passes
   210→- [ ] `spotlessCheck` passes
   211→
   212→## Step 12 – Retrospective: update this skill with what was learned
   213→
   214→After the instrumentation is complete (or abandoned), review the full session and improve this skill for future use.
   215→
   216→**Collect lessons from four sources:**
   217→
   218→1. **Build/test failures** — did any Gradle task fail with an error that this skill did not anticipate or gave wrong
   219→   guidance for? (e.g. a muzzle failure that wasn't caused by missing helpers, a test pattern that didn't work)
   220→2. **Docs vs. skill gaps** — did Step 1's sync miss anything? Did you consult the docs for something not captured here?
   221→3. **Reference instrumentation insights** — did the reference integration use a pattern, API, or convention not
   222→   reflected in any step of this skill?
   223→4. **User corrections** — did the user correct an output, override a decision, or point out a mistake?
   224→
   225→**For each lesson identified**, edit this file (`.claude/skills/apm-integrations/SKILL.md`) using the `Edit` tool:
   226→- Wrong rule → fix it in place
   227→- Missing rule → add it to the most relevant step
   228→- Wrong failure guidance → update the relevant "If X fails" section in Step 10
   229→- Misleading or obsolete content → remove it
   230→
   231→Keep each change minimal and targeted. Do not rewrite sections that worked correctly.
   232→After editing, confirm to the user which improvements were made to the skill.
   233→

content.content:
1→# How Instrumentations Work
     2→
     3→## Introduction
     4→
     5→Around 120 integrations consisting of about 200 instrumentations are currently provided with the Datadog Java Trace
     6→Agent.
     7→An auto-instrumentation allows compiled Java applications to be instrumented at runtime by a Java agent.
     8→This happens when compiled classes matching rules defined in the instrumentation undergo bytecode manipulation to
     9→accomplish some of what could be done by a developer instrumenting the code manually.
    10→Instrumentations are maintained in `/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/`
    11→
    12→## Files/Directories
    13→
    14→Instrumentations are in the directory:
    15→
    16→`/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/$framework/$framework-$minVersion`
    17→
    18→where `$framework` is the framework name, and `$minVersion` is the minimum version of the framework supported by the
    19→instrumentation.
    20→For example:
    21→
    22→```
    23→$ tree dd-java-agent/instrumentation/couchbase -L 2
    24→dd-java-agent/instrumentation/couchbase
    25→├── couchbase-2.0
    26→│   ├── build.gradle
    27→│   └── src
    28→├── couchbase-2.6
    29→│   ├── build.gradle
    30→│   └── src
    31→├── couchbase-3.1
    32→│   ├── build.gradle
    33→│   └── src
    34→└── couchbase-3.2
    35→    ├── build.gradle
    36→    └── src
    37→```
    38→
    39→In some cases, such as [Hibernate](../dd-java-agent/instrumentation/hibernate), there is a submodule containing
    40→different version-specific instrumentations, but typically a version-specific module is enough when there is only one
    41→instrumentation implemented (e.g. [Akka-HTTP](../dd-java-agent/instrumentation/akka/akka-http/akka-http-10.0))
    42→
    43→## Gradle
    44→
    45→Instrumentations included when building the Datadog java trace agent are defined in
    46→[`/settings.gradle`](../settings.gradle.kts) in alphabetical order with the other instrumentations in this format:
    47→
    48→```kotlin
    49→include(":dd-java-agent:instrumentation:<framework>:<framework>-<minVersion>")
    50→```
    51→
    52→Dependencies specific to a particular instrumentation are added to the `build.gradle` file in that instrumentation’s
    53→directory.
    54→Declare necessary dependencies under `compileOnly` configuration so they do not leak into the agent jar.
    55→
    56→## Muzzle
    57→
    58→Muzzle directives are applied at build time from the `build.gradle` file.
    59→OpenTelemetry provides some [Muzzle documentation](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-java-instrumentation/blob/main/docs/contributing/muzzle.md).
    60→Muzzle directives check for a range of framework versions that are safe to load the instrumentation.
    61→
    62→See this excerpt as an example from [rediscala](../dd-java-agent/instrumentation/rediscala-1.8/build.gradle):
    63→
    64→```groovy
    65→muzzle {
    66→  pass {
    67→    group = "com.github.etaty"
    68→    module = "rediscala_2.11"
    69→    versions = "[1.5.0,)"
    70→    assertInverse = true
    71→  }
    72→
    73→  pass {
    74→    group = "com.github.etaty"
    75→    module = "rediscala_2.12"
    76→    versions = "[1.8.0,)"
    77→    assertInverse = true
    78→  }
    79→}
    80→```
    81→
    82→This means that the instrumentation should be safe with `rediscala_2.11` from version `1.5.0` and all later versions,
    83→but should fail (and so will not be loaded), for older versions (see `assertInverse`).
    84→A similar range of versions is specified for `rediscala_2.12`.
    85→When the agent is built, the muzzle plugin will download versions of the framework and check these directives hold.
    86→To run muzzle on your instrumentation, run:
    87→
    88→```shell
    89→./gradlew :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:rediscala-1.8:muzzle
    90→```
    91→
    92→> [!WARNING]
    93→> Muzzle does _not_ run tests.
    94→> It checks that the types and methods used by the instrumentation are present in particular versions of libraries.
    95→> It can be subverted with `MethodHandle` and reflection -- in other words, having the `muzzle` task passing is not enough
    96→> to validate an instrumentation.
    97→
    98→By default, all the muzzle directives are checked against all the instrumentations included in a module.
    99→However, there can be situations in which it's only needed to check one specific directive on an instrumentation.
   100→At this point the instrumentation should override the method `muzzleDirective()` by returning the name of the directive to execute.
   101→
   102→### Identifying Breaking Changes with JApiCmp
   103→
   104→Before defining muzzle version ranges, you can use the JApiCmp plugin to compare different versions of a library and
   105→identify breaking API changes. This helps determine where to split version ranges in your muzzle directives.
   106→
   107→The `japicmp` task compares two versions of a Maven artifact and reports:
   108→- Removed classes and methods (breaking changes)
   109→- Added classes and methods (non-breaking changes)
   110→- Modified methods with binary compatibility status
   111→
   112→#### Usage
   113→
   114→Compare two versions of any Maven artifact:
   115→
   116→```shell
   117→./gradlew japicmp -Partifact=groupId:artifactId -Pbaseline=oldVersion -Ptarget=newVersion
   118→```
   119→
   120→For example, to compare MongoDB driver versions:
   121→
   122→```shell
   123→./gradlew japicmp -Partifact=org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync -Pbaseline=3.11.0 -Ptarget=4.0.0
   124→```
   125→
   126→#### Output
   127→
   128→The task generates two reports:
   129→
   130→- **Text report**: `build/reports/japicmp.txt` - Detailed line-by-line comparison
   131→- **HTML report**: `build/reports/japicmp.html` - Browsable visual report
   132→
   133→## Instrumentation classes
   134→
   135→The Instrumentation class is where the instrumentation begins. It will:
   136→
   137→1. Use Matchers to choose target types (i.e., classes)
   138→2. From only those target types, use Matchers to select the members (i.e., methods) to instrument.
   139→3. Apply instrumentation code from an Advice class to those members.
   140→
   141→Instrumentation classes:
   142→
   143→1. Must be annotated with `@AutoService(InstrumenterModule.class)`
   144→2. Should be declared in a file that ends with `Instrumentation.java`
   145→3. Should extend one of the six abstract TargetSystem `InstrumenterModule` classes
   146→4. Should implement one of the `Instrumenter` interfaces
   147→
   148→For example:
   149→
   150→```java
   151→import datadog.trace.agent.tooling.Instrumenter;
   152→import datadog.trace.agent.tooling.InstrumenterModule;
   153→
   154→@AutoService(InstrumenterModule.class)
   155→public class RabbitChannelInstrumentation extends InstrumenterModule.Tracing
   156→        implements Instrumenter.ForTypeHierarchy {/* */
   157→}
   158→```
   159→
   160→|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |                                                                                                            |
   161→|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
   162→| **TargetSystem**                                                                                                                                                                                                                 | **Usage**                                                                                                  |
   163→| `InstrumenterModule.`[`Tracing`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java#L184)      | An Instrumentation class should extend an appropriate provided TargetSystem class when possible.           |
   164→| `InstrumenterModule.`[`Profiling`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java#L196)    |                                                                                                            |
   165→| `InstrumenterModule.`[`AppSec`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java#L215)       |                                                                                                            |
   166→| `InstrumenterModule.`[`Iast`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java#L228)         |                                                                                                            |
   167→| `InstrumenterModule.`[`CiVisibility`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java#L285) |                                                                                                            |
   168→| `InstrumenterModule.`[`Usm`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java#L273)          |                                                                                                            |
   169→| `InstrumenterModule.`[`ContextTracking`]()                                                                                                                                                                                       | For instrumentations that only track context propagation without creating tracing spans.                   |
   170→| [`InstrumenterModule`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java)                     | Avoid extending `InstrumenterModule` directly.  When no other TargetGroup is applicable we generally default to `InstrumenterModule.Tracing` |
   171→
   172→### Grouping Instrumentations
   173→
   174→Related instrumentations may be grouped under a single `InstrumenterModule` to share common details such as integration
   175→name, helpers, context store use, and optional `classLoaderMatcher()`.
   176→
   177→Module classes:
   178→
   179→1. Must be annotated with `@AutoService(InstrumenterModule.class)`
   180→2. Should be declared in a file that ends with `Module.java`
   181→3. Should extend one of the six abstract TargetSystem `InstrumenterModule` classes
   182→4. Should have a `typeInstrumentations()` method that returns the instrumentations in the group
   183→5. Should NOT implement one of the `Instrumenter` interfaces
   184→
   185→> [!WARNING]
   186→> Grouped instrumentations must NOT be annotated with `@AutoService(InstrumenterModule.class)
   187→> and must NOT extend any of the six abstract `TargetSystem` `InstrumenterModule` classes.
   188→
   189→Existing instrumentations can be grouped under a new module, assuming they share the same integration name.
   190→
   191→For each member instrumentation:
   192→
   193→1. Remove `@AutoService(InstrumenterModule.class)`
   194→2. Remove `extends InstrumenterModule...`
   195→3. Move the list of helpers to the module, merging as necessary
   196→4. Move the context store map to the module, merging as necessary
   197→
   198→### Type Matching
   199→
   200→Instrumentation classes should implement an
   201→appropriate [Instrumenter interface](../dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/Instrumenter.java)
   202→that specifies how target types will be selected for instrumentation.
   203→
   204→|                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |                                                                                    |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
   205→|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
   206→| **Instrumenter Interface**                                                                                                                                                                                          | **Method(s)**                                                                      | **Usage(Example)**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
   207→| [`ForSingleType`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/297b575f0f265c1dc78f9958e7b4b9365c80d1f9/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/Instrumenter.java#L70)               | `String instrumentedType()`                                                        | Instruments only a single class name known at compile time.(see [Json2FactoryInstrumentation](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/9a28dc3f0333e781b2defc378c9020bf0a44ee9a/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/jackson-core/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/jackson/core/Json2FactoryInstrumentation.java#L19))                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |

   219→
   220→Consider adding an
   221→appropriate [ClassLoaderMatcher](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/82a3400cd210f4051b92fe1a86cd1b64a17e005e/dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/InstrumenterModule.java#L137)
   222→so the Instrumentation only activates when that class is loaded. For example:
   223→
   224→```java
   225→@Override
   226→public ElementMatcher<ClassLoader> classLoaderMatcher() {
   227→    return hasClassNamed("java.net.http.HttpClient");
   228→}
   229→```
   230→
   231→The `Instrumenter.ForBootstrap` interface is a hint that this instrumenter works on bootstrap types and there is no
   232→classloader present to interrogate. Use it when instrumenting something from the JDK that will be on the bootstrap
   233→classpath. For
   234→example, [`ShutdownInstrumentation`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/3e81c006b54f73aae61f88c39b52a7267267075b/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/shutdown/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/shutdown/ShutdownInstrumentation.java#L18)
   235→or [`UrlInstrumentation`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/3e81c006b54f73aae61f88c39b52a7267267075b/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/http-url-connection/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/http_url_connection/UrlInstrumentation.java#L21).
   236→
   237→> [!NOTE]
   238→> Without classloader available, helper classes for bootstrap instrumentation must be place into the 
   239→> `:dd-java-agent:agent-bootstrap` module rather than loaded using [the default mechanism](#helper-classes). 
   240→
   241→### Method Matching
   242→
   243→After the type is selected, the type’s target members(e.g., methods) must next be selected using the Instrumentation
   244→class’s `adviceTransformations()` method.
   245→ByteBuddy’s [`ElementMatchers`](https://javadoc.io/doc/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.4.17/net/bytebuddy/matcher/ElementMatchers.html)
   246→are used to describe the target members to be instrumented.
   247→Datadog’s [`DDElementMatchers`](../dd-java-agent/agent-builder/src/main/java/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/bytebuddy/matcher/DDElementMatchers.java)
   248→class also provides these 10 additional matchers:
   249→
   250→* implementsInterface
   251→* hasInterface
   252→* hasSuperType
   253→* declaresMethod
   254→* extendsClass
   255→* concreteClass
   256→* declaresField
   257→* declaresContextField
   258→* declaresAnnotation
   259→* hasSuperMethod
   260→
   261→Here, any public `execute()` method taking no arguments will have `PreparedStatementAdvice` applied:
   262→
   263→```java
   264→@Override
   265→public void adviceTransformations(AdviceTransformation transformation) {
   266→    transformation.applyAdvice(
   267→            nameStartsWith("execute")
   268→                    .and(takesArguments(0))
   269→                    .and(isPublic()),
   270→            getClass().getName() + "$PreparedStatementAdvice"
   271→    );
   272→}
   273→```
   274→
   275→Here, any matching `connect()` method will have `DriverAdvice` applied:
   276→
   277→```java
   278→@Override
   279→public void adviceTransformations(AdviceTransformation transformation) {
   280→    transformation.applyAdvice(
   281→            nameStartsWith("connect")
   282→                    .and(takesArgument(0, String.class))
   283→                    .and(takesArgument(1, Properties.class))
   284→                    .and(returns(named("java.sql.Connection"))),
   285→            getClass().getName() + "$DriverAdvice");
   286→}
   287→```
   288→
   289→### Applying Multiple Advice Classes
   290→
   291→The `applyAdvices` method supports applying multiple advice classes to the same method matcher using varargs. This is useful when you need to apply different advices for different target systems:
   292→
   293→```java
   294→@Override
   295→public void adviceTransformations(AdviceTransformation transformation) {
   296→    transformation.applyAdvices(
   297→            named("service")
   298→                    .and(takesArgument(0, named("org.apache.coyote.Request")))
   299→                    .and(takesArgument(1, named("org.apache.coyote.Response"))),
   300→            getClass().getName() + "$ContextTrackingAdvice",  // Applied first
   301→            getClass().getName() + "$ServiceAdvice"           // Applied second
   302→    );
   303→}
   304→```
   305→
   306→When multiple advices are specified, they are applied in the order they are listed. The agent will check each advice's target system compatibility (see [@AppliesOn annotation](#applieson-annotation)) and only apply advices that match the enabled target systems.
   307→
   308→Be precise in matching to avoid inadvertently instrumenting something unintended in a current or future version of the target class.
   309→Having multiple precise matchers is preferable to one more vague catch-all matcher which leaves some method characteristics undefined.
   310→
   311→Instrumentation class names should end in _Instrumentation._
   312→
   313→## Helper Classes
   314→
   315→Classes referenced by Advice that are not provided on the bootclasspath must be defined in Helper Classes otherwise they
   316→will not be loaded at runtime.
   317→This includes any decorators, extractors/injectors, or wrapping classes such as tracing listeners that extend or implement
   318→types provided by the library being instrumented. Also watch out for implicit types such as anonymous/nested classes
   319→because they must be listed alongside the main helper class.
   320→
   321→If an instrumentation is producing no results it may be that a required class is missing. Running muzzle
   322→
   323→```shell
   324→./gradlew muzzle
   325→```
   326→
   327→can quickly tell you if you missed a required helper class.
   328→Messages like this in debug logs also indicate that classes are missing:
   329→
   330→```
   331→[MSC service thread 1-3] DEBUG datadog.trace.agent.tooling.muzzle.MuzzleCheck - Muzzled mismatch - instrumentation.names=[jakarta-mdb] instrumentation.class=datadog.trace.instrumentation.jakarta.jms.MDBMessageConsumerInstrumentation instrumentation.target.classloader=ModuleClassLoader for Module "deployment.cmt.war" from Service Module Loader muzzle.mismatch="datadog.trace.instrumentation.jakarta.jms.MessageExtractAdapter:20 Missing class datadog.trace.instrumentation.jakarta.jms.MessageExtractAdapter$1"
   332→```
   333→
   334→The missing class must be added in the helperClassNames method, for example:
   335→
   336→```java
   337→@Override
   338→public String[] helperClassNames() {
   339→    return new String[]{
   340→            "datadog.trace.instrumentation.jakarta.jms.MessageExtractAdapter",
   341→            "datadog.trace.instrumentation.jakarta.jms.JMSDecorator",
   342→            "datadog.trace.instrumentation.jakarta.jms.MessageExtractAdapter$1"
   343→    };
   344→}
   345→```
   346→
   347→## Enums
   348→
   349→Use care when deciding to include enums in your Advice and Decorator classes because each element of the enum will need
   350→to be added to the helper classes individually.
   351→For example not just `MyDecorator.MyEnum` but also `MyDecorator.MyEnum$1, MyDecorator.MyEnum$2`, etc.

   391→## Advice Classes
   392→
   393→Byte Buddy injects compiled bytecode at runtime to wrap existing methods, so they communicate with Datadog at entry or exit.
   394→These modifications are referred to as _advice transformation_ or just _advice_.
   395→
   396→Instrumenters register advice transformations by calling `AdviceTransformation.applyAdvice(ElementMatcher, String)` 
   397→and Methods are matched by the instrumentation's `adviceTransformations()` method.
   398→
   399→The Advice is injected into the type so Advice can only refer to those classes on the bootstrap class-path or helpers
   400→injected into the application class-loader.
   401→Advice must not refer to any methods in the instrumentation class or even other methods in the same advice class because
   402→the advice is really only a template of bytecode to be inserted into the target class.
   403→It is only the advice bytecode (plus helpers) that is copied over.
   404→The rest of the instrumenter and advice class is ignored.
   405→Do not place code in the Advice constructor because the constructor is never called.
   406→
   407→You can not use methods like `InstrumentationContext.get()` outside of the instrumentation advice because the tracer
   408→currently patches the method stub with the real call at runtime.
   409→But you can pass the ContextStore into a helper/decorator like in [DatadogMessageListener](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/743bacde52ba4369e05631436168bfde9b815c8b/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/jms/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/jms/DatadogMessageListener.java).
   410→This could reduce duplication if you re-used the helper.
   411→But unlike most applications, some duplication can be the better choice in the tracer if it simplifies things and reduces overhead.
   412→You might end up with very similar code scattered around, but it will be simple to maintain.
   413→Trying to find an abstraction that works well across instrumentations can take time and may introduce extra indirection.
   414→
   415→Advice classes provide the code to be executed before and/or after a matched method.
   416→The classes use a static method annotated by `@Advice.OnMethodEnter` and/or `@Advice.OnMethodExit` to provide the code.
   417→The method name is irrelevant.
   418→
   419→A method that is annotated with `@Advice.OnMethodEnter `can annotate its parameters with `@Advice.Argument`.
   420→`@Advice.Argument` will substitute this parameter with the corresponding argument of the instrumented method.
   421→This allows the `@Advice.OnMethodEnter` code to see and modify the parameters that would be passed to the target method.
   422→
   423→Alternatively, a parameter can be annotated by `Advice.This` where the `this` reference of the instrumented method is
   424→assigned to the new parameter.
   425→This can also be used to assign a new value to the `this` reference of an instrumented method.
   426→
   427→If no annotation is used on a parameter, it is assigned the n-th parameter of the instrumented method for the n-th
   428→parameter of the advice method.
   429→Explicitly specifying which parameter is intended is recommended to be more clear, for example:
   430→
   431→`@Advice.Argument(0) final HttpUriRequest request`
   432→
   433→All parameters must declare the exact same type as the parameters of the instrumented type or the method's declaring
   434→type for `Advice.This`.
   435→If they are marked as read-only, then the parameter type may be a super type of the original.
   436→
   437→A method that is annotated with `Advice.OnMethodExit` can also annotate its parameters with `Advice.Argument`
   438→and `Advice.This`.
   439→It can also annotate a parameter with `Advice.Return` to receive the original method's return value.
   440→By reassigning the return value, it can replace the returned value.
   441→If an instrumented method does not return a value, this annotation must not be used.
   442→If a method throws an exception, the parameter is set to its default value (0 for primitive types and null for reference types).
   443→The parameter's type must equal the instrumented method's return type if it is not set to read-only.
   444→If the parameter is read-only it may be a super type of the instrumented method's return type.
   445→
   446→Advice class names should end in _Advice._
   447→
   448→### @AppliesOn Annotation
   449→
   450→The `@AppliesOn` annotation allows you to override which target systems a specific advice class applies to, independent of the InstrumenterModule's target system. This is useful when you have an instrumentation module that extends one target system (e.g., `InstrumenterModule.Tracing`), but want certain advice classes to also be applied for other target systems.
   451→
   452→#### Usage
   453→
   454→Annotate your advice class with `@AppliesOn` and specify the target systems where this advice should be applied:
   455→
   456→```java
   457→import datadog.trace.agent.tooling.InstrumenterModule.TargetSystem;
   458→import datadog.trace.agent.tooling.annotation.AppliesOn;
   459→
   460→@AppliesOn(TargetSystem.CONTEXT_TRACKING)
   461→public static class ContextTrackingAdvice {
   462→    @Advice.OnMethodEnter(suppress = Throwable.class)
   463→    public static void extractParent(
   464→            @Advice.Argument(0) org.apache.coyote.Request req,
   465→            @Advice.Local("parentScope") ContextScope parentScope) {
   466→        // This advice only runs when CONTEXT_TRACKING is enabled
   467→        final Context parentContext = DECORATE.extract(req);
   468→        parentScope = parentContext.attach();
   469→    }
   470→
   471→    @Advice.OnMethodExit(suppress = Throwable.class, onThrowable = Throwable.class)
   472→    public static void closeScope(@Advice.Local("parentScope") ContextScope scope) {
   473→        scope.close();
   474→    }
   475→}
   476→```
   477→
   478→#### When to Use @AppliesOn
   479→
   480→Use `@AppliesOn` when:
   481→
   482→1. **Selective Advice Application**: You want different advice classes within the same instrumentation to apply to different target systems. For example, an instrumentation might extend `InstrumenterModule.Tracing` but have some advice that should only run for `CONTEXT_TRACKING`.
   483→
   484→2. **Multi-System Support**: Your instrumentation needs to work across multiple target systems with different behaviors for each. By applying multiple advices with different `@AppliesOn` annotations, you can customize behavior per target system.
   485→
   486→3. **Separating Concerns**: You want to cleanly separate context tracking logic from tracing logic in the same instrumentation, making the code more maintainable.
   487→
   488→#### Example: Tomcat Server Instrumentation
   489→
   490→In the Tomcat instrumentation, we apply both context tracking and tracing advices to the same method:
   491→
   492→```java
   493→@Override
   494→public void adviceTransformations(AdviceTransformation transformation) {
   495→    transformation.applyAdvices(
   496→            named("service")
   497→                    .and(takesArgument(0, named("org.apache.coyote.Request")))
   498→                    .and(takesArgument(1, named("org.apache.coyote.Response"))),
   499→            TomcatServerInstrumentation.class.getName() + "$ContextTrackingAdvice",
   500→            TomcatServerInstrumentation.class.getName() + "$ServiceAdvice"
   501→    );
   502→}
   503→```
   504→
   505→The `ContextTrackingAdvice` is annotated with `@AppliesOn(TargetSystem.CONTEXT_TRACKING)`, so it only runs when context tracking is enabled. The `ServiceAdvice` (without the annotation) runs when the module's target system (`TRACING`) is enabled.
   506→
   507→#### Important Notes
   508→
   509→- If an advice class does not have the `@AppliesOn` annotation, it will be applied whenever the parent InstrumenterModule's target system is enabled.
   510→- When multiple advices are applied to the same method, they are applied in the order specified, and each one's target system compatibility is checked individually.
   511→
   512→## Exceptions in Advice
   513→
   514→Advice methods are typically annotated like
   515→
   516→`@Advice.OnMethodEnter(suppress = Throwable.class)`
   517→
   518→and
   519→
   520→`@Advice.OnMethodExit(onThrowable = Throwable.class, suppress = Throwable.class)`
   521→
   522→Using `suppress = Throwable.class` is considered our default for both methods unless there is a reason not to suppress.
   523→It means the exception handler is triggered on any exception thrown within the Advice, which terminates the Advice method.
   524→The opposite would be either no `suppress` annotation or equivalently `suppress = NoExceptionHandler.class` which would 
   525→allow exceptions in Advice code to surface and is usually undesirable.
   526→
   527→> [!NOTE]
   528→> Don't use `suppress` on an advice hooking a constructor.
   529→> For older JVMs that do not support [flexible constructor bodies](https://openjdk.org/jeps/513), you can't decorate the
   530→> mandatory self or parent constructor call with try/catch, as it must be the first call from the constructor body.
   531→
   532→If
   533→the [`Advice.OnMethodEnter`](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.OnMethodEnter.html)
   534→method throws an exception,
   535→the [`Advice.OnMethodExit`](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.OnMethodExit.html)
   536→method is not invoked.
   537→
   538→The [`Advice.Thrown`](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.Thrown.html)
   539→annotation passes any thrown exception from the instrumented method to
   540→the [`Advice.OnMethodExit`](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.OnMethodExit.html)
   541→advice
   542→method.  
   543→[`Advice.Thrown`](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.Thrown.html) ****
   544→should annotate at most one parameter on the exit advice.
   545→
   546→If the instrumented method throws an exception,
   547→the [Advice.OnMethodExit](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.OnMethodExit.html)
   548→method is still invoked unless
   549→the [Advice.OnMethodExit.onThrowable()](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.OnMethodExit.html#onThrowable--)
   550→property is set to false. If this property is set to false,
   551→the [Advice.Thrown](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.Thrown.html)
   552→annotation must not be used on any parameter.
   553→
   554→If an instrumented method throws an exception, the return parameter is set to its default of 0 for primitive types or
   555→null for reference types.
   556→An exception can be read by annotating an exit
   557→method’s [Throwable](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/1.5.0/docs/api/java/lang/Throwable.html?is-external=true) parameter
   558→with [Advice.Thrown](https://javadoc.io/static/net.bytebuddy/byte-buddy/1.10.2/net/bytebuddy/asm/Advice.Thrown.html)
   559→which is assigned the
   560→thrown [Throwable](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/1.5.0/docs/api/java/lang/Throwable.html?is-external=true) or null if a
   561→method returns normally. This allows exchanging a thrown exception with any checked or unchecked exception.
   562→For example, either the result or the exception will be passed to the helper method here:
   563→
   564→```java
   565→@Advice.OnMethodExit(onThrowable = Throwable.class, suppress = Throwable.class)
   566→public static void methodExit(
   567→        @Advice.Return final Object result,
   568→        @Advice.Thrown final Throwable throwable
   569→) {
   570→    HelperMethods.doMethodExit(result, throwable);
   571→}
   572→```
   573→
   574→## Logging in Instrumentations
   575→
   576→Logging should only be used in helper classes where you can easily add and access a static logger field:
   577→
   578→```java
   579→// GOOD - Logger only in helper classes
   580→public class MyInstrumentationHelper {
   581→  private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MyInstrumentationHelper.class);
   582→
   583→  public void helperMethod() {
   584→    log.debug("Logging from helper is safe");
   585→    // This helper is called from instrumentation/advice
   586→  }
   587→}
   588→```
   589→
   590→`org.slf4j` is the logging facade to use.
   591→It is shaded and redirects to our internal logger.
   592→
   593→> [!CAUTION]
   594→> Do NOT put logger fields in instrumentation classes:
   595→
   596→```java
   597→// BAD - Logger in instrumentation class
   598→public class MyInstrumentation extends InstrumenterModule.Tracing {
   599→  private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MyInstrumentation.class);
   600→}
   601→```
   602→
   603→> [!CAUTION]
   604→> Do NOT put logger fields in Advice classes:
   605→
   606→```java
   607→// BAD - Logger in advice class
   608→public class MyAdvice {
   609→  private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MyAdvice.class);
   610→
   611→  @Advice.OnMethodEnter(suppress = Throwable.class)
   612→  public static void enter() {
   613→    log.debug("Entering method"); // BAD
   614→  }
   615→}
   616→```
   617→
   618→## InjectAdapters & Custom GETTERs/SETTERs
   619→
   620→Custom Inject Adapter static instances typically named `SETTER` implement the `AgentPropagation.Setter` interface and
   621→are used to normalize setting shared context values such as in HTTP headers.
   622→
   623→Custom inject adapter static instances typically named `GETTER` implement the `AgentPropagation.Getter` interface and
   624→are used to normalize extracting shared context values such as from HTTP headers.
   625→
   626→For example `google-http-client` sets its header values using:
   627→
   628→`com.google.api.client.http.HttpRequest.getHeaders().put(key,value)`
   629→
   630→```java
   631→package datadog.trace.instrumentation.googlehttpclient;
   632→
   633→import com.google.api.client.http.HttpRequest;
   634→import datadog.trace.bootstrap.instrumentation.api.AgentPropagation;
   635→
   636→public class HeadersInjectAdapter implements AgentPropagation.Setter<HttpRequest> {
   637→    public static final HeadersInjectAdapter SETTER = new HeadersInjectAdapter();
   638→
   639→    @Override
   640→    public void set(final HttpRequest carrier, final String key, final String value) {
   641→        carrier.getHeaders().put(key, value);
   642→    }
   643→}
   644→```
   645→
   646→But notice `apache-http-client5` sets its header values using:
   647→
   648→`org.apache.hc.core5.http.HttpRequest.setHeader(key,value)`
   649→
   650→```java
   651→package datadog.trace.instrumentation.apachehttpclient5;
   652→
   653→import datadog.trace.bootstrap.instrumentation.api.AgentPropagation;
   654→import org.apache.hc.core5.http.HttpRequest;
   655→
   656→public class HttpHeadersInjectAdapter implements AgentPropagation.Setter<HttpRequest> {
   657→    public static final HttpHeadersInjectAdapter SETTER = new HttpHeadersInjectAdapter();
   658→
   659→    @Override
   660→    public void set(final HttpRequest carrier, final String key, final String value) {
   661→        carrier.setHeader(key, value);
   662→    }
   663→}
   664→```
   665→
   666→These implementation-specific methods are both wrapped in a standard set(...) method by the SETTER.
   667→
   668→## To Wrap or Not To Wrap?
   669→
   670→Typically, an instrumentation will use ByteBuddy to apply new code from an Advice class before and/or after the targeted
   671→code using `@Advice.OnMethodEnter` and `@Advice.OnMethodExit.`
   672→
   673→Alternatively, you can replace the call to the target method with your own code which wraps the original method call.
   674→An example is the JMS Instrumentation which replaces the `MessageListener.onMessage()` method
   675→with `DatadogMessageListener.onMessage()`. 
   676→The `DatadogMessageListener` then [calls the original `onMessage()` method](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/9a28dc3f0333e781b2defc378c9020bf0a44ee9a/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/jms/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/jms/DatadogMessageListener.java#L73).
   677→Note that this style is **_not recommended_** because it can cause datadog packages to appear in stack traces generated
   678→by errors in user code. This has created confusion in the past.
   679→
   680→## Context Stores
   681→
   682→Context stores pass information between instrumented methods, using library objects that both methods have access to.
   683→They can be used to attach data to a request when the request is received, and read that data where the request is
   684→deserialized.
   685→Context stores work internally by dynamically adding a field to the “carrier” object by manipulating the bytecode.
   686→Since they manipulate bytecode, context stores can only be created within Advice classes.
   687→For example:
   688→
   689→```java
   690→ContextStore<X> store = InstrumentationContext.get(
   691→        "com.amazonaws.services.sqs.model.ReceiveMessageResult", "java.lang.String");
   692→```
   693→
   694→It’s also possible to pass the types as class objects, but this is only possible for classes that are in the bootstrap
   695→classpath.
   696→Basic types like `String` would work and the usual datadog types like `AgentSpan` are OK too, but classes from the
   697→library you are instrumenting are not.
   698→
   699→In the example above, that context store is used to store an arbitrary `String` in a `ReceiveMessageResult` class.
   700→It is used like a Map:
   701→
   702→```java
   703→store.put(response, "my string");
   704→```
   705→
   706→and/or
   707→
   708→```java
   709→String stored = store.get(response); // "my string"
   710→```
   711→
   712→Context stores also need to be pre-declared in the Advice by overriding the `contextStore()` method otherwise, using
   713→them throws exceptions.
   714→
   715→```java
   716→@Override
   717→public Map<String, String> contextStore() {
   718→    return singletonMap(
   719→            "com.amazonaws.services.sqs.model.ReceiveMessageResult",
   720→            "java.lang.String"
   721→    );
   722→}
   723→```
   724→
   725→It is important to understand that even though they look like maps, since the value is stored in the key, you can only
   726→retrieve a value if you use the exact same key object as when it was set.
   727→Using a different object that is “`.equals()`” to the first will yield nothing.
   728→
   729→Since `ContextStore` does not support null keys, null checks must be enforced _before_ using an object as a key.
   730→
   731→## CallDepthThreadLocalMap
   732→
   733→In order to avoid activating new spans on recursive calls to the same method
   734→a [CallDepthThreadLocalMap](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/9d5c7ea524cfec982176e687a489fc8c2865e445/dd-java-agent/agent-bootstrap/src/main/java/datadog/trace/bootstrap/CallDepthThreadLocalMap.java#L11)
   735→is often used to determine if a call is recursive by using a counter. It is incremented with each call to the method
   736→and [decremented](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/9d5c7ea524cfec982176e687a489fc8c2865e445/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/vertx-redis-client-3.9/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/vertx_redis_client/RedisSendAdvice.java#L82) (
   737→or [reset](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/9d5c7ea524cfec982176e687a489fc8c2865e445/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/java-http-client/src/main/java11/datadog/trace/instrumentation/httpclient/SendAdvice.java#L44))
   738→when exiting.
   739→
   740→This only works if the methods are called on the same thread since the counter is a ThreadLocal variable.
   741→
   742→## Span Lifecycle
   743→
   744→In Advice classes, the `@Advice.OnMethodEnter` methods typically start spans and `@Advice.OnMethodExit` methods
   745→typically finish spans.
   746→
   747→Starting the span may be done directly or with helper methods which eventually make a call to one of the
   748→various `AgentTracer.startSpan(...)` methods.
   749→
   750→Finishing the span is normally done by calling `span.finish()` in the exit method;
   751→
   752→The basic span lifecycle in an Advice class looks like:
   753→
   754→1. Start the span
   755→2. Decorate the span
   756→3. Activate the span and get the AgentScope
   757→4. Run the instrumented target method
   758→5. Close the Agent Scope
   759→6. Finish the span
   760→
   761→```java
   762→@Advice.OnMethodEnter(suppress = Throwable.class)
   763→public static AgentScope begin() {
   764→    final AgentSpan span = startSpan(/* */);
   765→    DECORATE.afterStart(span);
   766→    return activateSpan(span);
   767→}
   768→
   769→@Advice.OnMethodExit(suppress = Throwable.class)
   770→public static void end(@Advice.Enter final AgentScope scope) {
   771→    AgentSpan span = scope.span();
   772→    DECORATE.beforeFinish(span);
   773→    scope.close();
   774→    span.finish();
   775→}
   776→```
   777→
   778→For example,
   779→the [`HttpUrlConnectionInstrumentation`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/4d0b113c4c9dc23ef2a44d30952d38d09ff28ff3/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/http-url-connection/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/http_url_connection/HttpUrlConnectionInstrumentation.java#L26)
   780→class contains
   781→the [`HttpUrlConnectionAdvice`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/4d0b113c4c9dc23ef2a44d30952d38d09ff28ff3/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/http-url-connection/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/http_url_connection/HttpUrlConnectionInstrumentation.java#L66)
   782→class which calls
   783→the `HttpUrlState.`[`start`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/4d0b113c4c9dc23ef2a44d30952d38d09ff28ff3/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/http-url-connection/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/http_url_connection/HttpUrlConnectionInstrumentation.java#L84)`()`
   784→and `HttpUrlState.`[`finishSpan`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/4d0b113c4c9dc23ef2a44d30952d38d09ff28ff3/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/http-url-connection/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/http_url_connection/HttpUrlConnectionInstrumentation.java#L113)`()`
   785→methods.
   786→
   787→## Continuations
   788→
   789→- [`AgentScope.Continuation`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/09ac78ff0b54fbbbee0ab1c89c901d2043fda40b/dd-trace-api/src/main/java/datadog/trace/context/TraceScope.java#L47)
   790→  is used to pass context between threads.
   791→- Continuations must be either activated or canceled.
   792→- If a Continuation is activated it returns a TraceScope which must eventually be closed.
   793→- Only after all TraceScopes are closed and any non-activated Continuations are canceled may the Trace finally close.
   794→
   795→Notice
   796→in [`HttpClientRequestTracingHandler`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/3fe1b2d6010e50f61518fa25af3bdeb03ae7712b/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/netty-4.1/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/netty41/client/HttpClientRequestTracingHandler.java#L56)
   797→how the AgentScope.Continuation is used to obtain the `parentScope` which is
   798→finally [closed](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/3fe1b2d6010e50f61518fa25af3bdeb03ae7712b/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/netty-4.1/src/main/java/datadog/trace/instrumentation/netty41/client/HttpClientRequestTracingHandler.java#L111).
   799→
   800→## Naming
   801→
   802→### Gradle Module Names
   803→
   804→Instrumentation Gradle modules must follow these naming conventions (enforced by the `dd-trace-java.instrumentation-naming` plugin):
   805→
   806→1. **Version or Suffix Requirement**: Module names must end with either:
   807→   - A version number (e.g., `2.0`, `3.1`, `3.1.0`)
   808→   - A configured suffix (i.e.: `-common` for shared classes, or product dependent like `-iast`)
   809→
   810→   Examples:
   811→   - `couchbase-2.0` ✓
   812→   - `couchbase-3.1.0` ✓
   813→   - `couchbase-common` ✓
   814→   - `couchbase` ✗ (missing version or suffix)
   815→
   816→2. **Parent Directory Name**: Module names must contain their parent directory name.
   817→
   818→   Examples:
   819→   - Parent: `couchbase`, Module: `couchbase-2.0` ✓ (contains couchbase)
   820→   - Parent: `couchbase`, Module: `couch-2.0` ✗ 
   821→
   822→3. **Exclusions**: Modules under `:dd-java-agent:instrumentation:datadog` are automatically excluded from these rules
   823→since they are not related to a third party library version. 
   824→They contain instrumentation modules related to internal datadog features, and they are classified by product.
   825→Examples are: `trace-annotation` (supporting the `tracing` product) or `enable-wallclock-profiling`.
   826→
   827→The naming rules can be checked when running `./gradlew checkInstrumentationNaming`.
   828→
   829→### Class and Package Names
   830→
   831→- Instrumentation names use kebab case. For example: `google-http-client`
   832→- Instrumentation module name and package name should be consistent.
   833→  For example, the instrumentation `google-http-client` contains the `GoogleHttpClientInstrumentation` class in the
   834→  package `datadog.trace.instrumentation.googlehttpclient`.
   835→- As usual, class names should be nouns, in camel case with the first letter of each internal word capitalized.
   836→  Use whole words-avoid acronyms and abbreviations (unless the abbreviation is much more widely used than the long form,
   837→  such as URL or HTML).
   838→- Advice class names should end in _Advice._
   839→- Instrumentation class names should end in _Instrumentation._
   840→- Decorator class names should end in _Decorator._
   841→
   842→## Tooling
   843→
   844→### ignored\_class\_name.trie
   845→
   846→The file [ignored\_class\_name.trie](../dd-java-agent/agent-tooling/src/main/resources/datadog/trace/agent/tooling/bytebuddy/matcher/ignored_class_name.trie)
   847→lists classes that are to be globally ignored by matchers because they are unsafe, pointless or expensive to transform.
   848→If you notice an expected class is not being transformed, it may be covered by an entry in this list.
   849→
   850→## GraalVM
   851→
   852→Instrumentations running on GraalVM should avoid using reflection if possible.
   853→If reflection must be used the reflection usage should be added to
   854→`dd-java-agent/agent-bootstrap/src/main/resources/META-INF/native-image/com.datadoghq/dd-java-agent/reflect-config.json`.
   855→
   856→See [GraalVM configuration docs](https://www.graalvm.org/jdk17/reference-manual/native-image/dynamic-features/Reflection/#manual-configuration).
   857→
   858→## Testing
   859→
   860→### Instrumentation Tests
   861→
   862→Tests are written in Groovy using the [Spock framework](http://spockframework.org).
   863→For instrumentations, `InstrumentationSpecification` must be extended.
   864→For example, HTTP server frameworks use base tests which enforce consistency between different implementations
   865→(see [HttpServerTest](../dd-java-agent/testing/src/main/groovy/datadog/trace/agent/test/base/HttpServerTest.groovy)).
   866→When writing an instrumentation it is much faster to test just the instrumentation rather than build the entire project,
   867→for example:
   868→
   869→```shell
   870→./gradlew :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:play-ws:play-ws-2.1:test
   871→```
   872→
   873→Sometimes it is necessary to force Gradle to discard cached test results and [rerun all tasks](https://docs.gradle.org/current/userguide/command_line_interface.html#sec:rerun_tasks).
   874→
   875→```shell
   876→./gradle test --rerun-tasks
   877→```
   878→
   879→Running tests that require JDK-21 can use the `-PtestJvm=21` flag (if not installed, Gradle will provision them),
   880→for example:
   881→
   882→```shell
   883→./gradlew  :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:aerospike-4.0:allLatestDepTests -PtestJvm=21
   884→```
   885→
   886→> [!TIP]
   887→> The `testJvm` property also accept a path to a JVM home. E.g.
   888→> 
   889→> ```shell
   890→> /gradlew  :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:an-insturmentation:test -PtestJvm=~/.local/share/mise/installs/java/openjdk-26.0.0-loom+1/
   891→> ```
   892→
   893→### Latest Dependency Tests
   894→
   895→Adding a directive to the build file gives early warning when breaking changes are released by framework maintainers.
   896→For example, for Play 2.5, we download the latest dependency and run tests against it:
   897→
   898→```groovy
   899→latestDepTestCompile group: 'com.typesafe.play', name: 'play-java_2.11', version: '2.5.+'
   900→
   901→latestDepTestCompile group: 'com.typesafe.play', name: 'play-java-ws_2.11', version: '2.5.+'
   902→
   903→latestDepTestCompile(group: 'com.typesafe.play', name: 'play-test_2.11', version: '2.5.+') {
   904→  exclude group: 'org.eclipse.jetty.websocket', module: 'websocket-client'
   905→}
   906→```
   907→
   908→Dependency tests can be run like:
   909→
   910→```shell
   911→./gradlew :dd-java-agent:instrumentation:play-ws:play-ws-2.1:latestDepTest
   912→```
   913→
   914→### Additional Test Suites
   915→
   916→The file [dd-trace-java/gradle/test-suites.gradle](../gradle/test-suites.gradle)
   917→contains these macros for adding different test suites to individual instrumentation builds.
   918→Notice how `addTestSuite` and `addTestSuiteForDir` pass values to [`addTestSuiteExtendingForDir`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/c3ea017590f10941232bbb0f694525bf124d4b49/gradle/test-suites.gradle#L3)
   919→which configures the tests.
   920→
   921→```groovy
   922→ext.addTestSuite = (String testSuiteName) -> {
   923→  ext.addTestSuiteForDir(testSuiteName, testSuiteName)
   924→}
   925→
   926→ext.addTestSuiteForDir = (String testSuiteName, String dirName) -> {
   927→  ext.addTestSuiteExtendingForDir(testSuiteName, 'test', dirName)
   928→}
   929→
   930→ext.addTestSuiteExtendingForDir = (String testSuiteName, String parentSuiteName, String dirName) -> { /* */ }
   931→```
   932→
   933→For example:
   934→
   935→```groovy
   936→addTestSuite('latestDepTest')
   937→```
   938→
   939→Also, the forked test for latestDep is not run by default without declaring something like:
   940→
   941→```groovy
   942→addTestSuiteExtendingForDir('latestDepForkedTest', 'latestDepTest', 'test')
   943→```
   944→
   945→(also example [`vertx-web-3.5/build.gradle`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-java/blob/c3ea017590f10941232bbb0f694525bf124d4b49/dd-java-agent/instrumentation/vertx-web-3.5/build.gradle#L18)`)`
   946→
   947→### Smoke Tests
   948→
   949→In addition to unit tests, [Smoke tests](../dd-smoke-tests) may be needed.
   950→Smoke tests run with a real agent jar file set as the `javaagent`.
   951→These are optional and not all frameworks have them, but contributions are very welcome.
   952→
